---

## Bayesian Analysis

The key trap here is **base rate neglect** — the intuitive answer ("99.9% accurate test → ~99.9% chance") is wrong by roughly an order of magnitude.

### Setup

| Variable | Value |
|---|---|
| Prevalence P(disease) | 1/10,000 = 0.0001 |
| Sensitivity P(+\|disease) | 0.999 |
| False positive rate P(+\|no disease) | 1 − 0.999 = **0.001** |

### Bayes' Theorem

$$P(\text{disease} \mid +) = \frac{P(+|\text{disease}) \cdot P(\text{disease})}{P(+)}$$

**Denominator** (total probability of a positive result):

$$P(+) = (0.999)(0.0001) + (0.001)(0.9999)$$
$$= 0.0000999 + 0.0009999 = 0.0010998$$

**Posterior:**

$$P(\text{disease} \mid +) = \frac{0.0000999}{0.0010998} \approx \mathbf{9.1\%}$$

### Intuition via natural frequencies

Imagine **10,000 patients**:
- **1** has the condition → ~1 true positive
- **9,999** don't → ~**10 false positives** (0.1% of 9,999)

So among ~11 positive results, only 1 is a true positive: **≈ 1/11 ≈ 9.1%**

---

## Clinical Implication

**You should assign roughly 9% probability** — not 99.9%.

This is why screening tests for rare conditions almost always require confirmatory testing. The math is working correctly: a positive result here updates the prior from 0.01% → 9%, which is a **~900-fold increase** in probability. That's meaningful signal — but it's far from diagnostic certainty.

The false positives swamp the true positives because the **9,999 healthy people** each contribute a tiny 0.1% false positive chance, and collectively they outweigh the single diseased individual.
